elements.domSnippet: Wishlist

summary: Interactive elements, such as buttons, links, and inputs, must convey that they can be interacted with by using the proper semantics.
description:
**Cause:** 
Screen readers are unable to identify that the element is interactive due to missing or incorrect semantics.
**Effect:** 
Screen reader users may not find the content or may not realize that the the content is interactive.
**Suggested Solutions:**
Use native HTML interactive elements such as <*button*>, <*a*>, <*select*>, <*input*>. Alternatively, add the appropriate ARIA role that corresponds to the content purpose.

elements.domSnippet: Wishlist

summary: Keyboard users must be able to navigate to all the interactive elements on the page.
description:
**Cause:** 
The element is missing the right tags or has incorrect tags. 
**Effect:** 
The element is excluded from the keyboard tab sequence and is therefore inaccessible to keyboard users. 
**Suggested Solutions:**
Use one of these approaches: 
* Approach 1 (recommended): Add a tag that shows the element's purpose. Interactive elements don't need anything extra because they are focusable by nature. 
* Approach 2: Add a `tabindex` attribute with the value of `0` to the element.

description:
**Cause:**

**Effect:**

**Suggested Solutions:**
Use native HTML interactive elements such as <*button*>, <*a*>, <*select*>, <*input*>. Alternatively, add the appropriate ARIA role that corresponds to the content purpose.

elements.domSnippet: Search

summary: Elements that can be interacted with, such as buttons, links, and input fields, must have an accessible name. The name should indicate which information or action is required, and/or what will happen if they interact with it.
description:
**Cause:** 
The element does not have an accessible name to inform the screen reader of its purpose and function.
**Effect:** 
Assistive technologies use the accessible name to label, announce and trigger the element's action. If there's no valid accessible name, assistive technologies won't be able to understand the element correctly. 
**Suggested Solutions:**
Depending on the type of element, either add a descriptive text to the body of the tag or add the appropriate attributes to the tag.

axeSuggestedSolutions.summary:
Fix any of the following:
  Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  Element has no title attribute
  Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  Element does not have an explicit <label>
  Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

elements.domSnippet:
<!--NOTE: snippet was trimmed due to its size--><html style="scroll-behavior: unset;"><head><meta charset="UTF-8"><meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,initial-scale=1"><title>Demo Website</title><style>*, *::before, *::after {
  box-sizing: border-box;
}

/* A11Y-GEN3 reflow: overflow-x: hidden prevents horizontal scrolling, which causes
   the navigation menu and other fixed-width content to be clipped and cut off when
   the page is zoomed to 300% — violates WCAG 1.4.10 (AA) Reflow */
body {
  margin: 0;
  padding: 0;
  background: #fff;
  font-family: 'Google Sans', Arial, sans-serif;
  color: #202124;
  -webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;
  overflow-x: hidden;
}

.app {
  min-height: 100vh;
  display: flex;
  flex-direction: column;
}

main {
  flex: 1;
}

.skip-link {
  position: absolute;
  top: -40px;
  left: 0;
  background: #1a73e8;
  color: #fff;
  padding: 8px 16px;
  z-index: 9999;
  font-size: 14px;
  text-decoration: none;
  border-radius: 0 0 4px 0;
  transition: top 0.2s;
}

.skip-link:focus {
  top: 0;
}

a {
  color: inherit
